messages.content:
You are the pre-refactor Perplexity Research lane for a controlled benchmark. Answer the exact question below with current, source-backed technical research. Compare viable architecture patterns (at minimum shared-writer with optimistic concurrency, single-writer/command-log, partitioned/sharded writers, and read/write split or queue-mediated variants), explain provenance and conflict prevention, discuss Supabase/Postgres connection-budget mechanics, and recommend one architecture for GBrain. Distinguish facts from judgment and identify assumptions, tradeoffs, and what would change the recommendation. Cite material claims with direct source URLs. Treat the Brain packet as prior context and classify findings as new, changed, missing, contradictory, or confirming. Do not claim novelty from absence of search results. Return a decision-oriented memo, not a plan to modify files.

EXACT QUESTION:
What is the best architecture for multiple concurrent AI agents sharing a Supabase/Postgres-backed knowledge graph while preserving provenance, preventing conflicting writes, and staying within a strict database connection budget? Compare viable patterns and recommend one for GBrain.

BRAIN CONTEXT PACKET (use verbatim as the bounded prior context):
brain_context:
  retrieved_at: 2026-07-15T00:35:14Z
  brain_id: default
  source_id: gbrain-code
  query: "What is the best architecture for multiple concurrent AI agents sharing a Supabase/Postgres-backed knowledge graph while preserving provenance, preventing conflicting writes, and staying within a strict database connection budget? Compare viable patterns and recommend one for GBrain."
  status: thin_broadened
  search_scope:
    primary_query: "Supabase Postgres knowledge graph concurrent agents provenance connection budget"
    primary_result: "No results."
    broadened_queries:
      - "knowledge graph"
      - "provenance"
      - "Supabase"
      - "connection pool"
      - "concurrent agents shared database writes"
    novelty_boundary: "No direct prior Brain page answered this architecture question. The claims below are implementation context retrieved from the gbrain-code source; external findings must be labeled new, changed, missing, contradictory, or confirming against this packet. Absence from the checked Brain queries is not evidence of novelty."
  pages:
    - slug: src-core-connection-manager-ts
      title: "src/core/connection-manager.ts (typescript)"
      retrieved_via: search|get
      claims:
        - "The current connection manager routes reads through a Supabase transaction pooler (port 6543) with a default read pool size of 10 and routes DDL/bulk work through a direct/session path (port 5432) with a default direct pool size of 3."
        - "Direct-pool initialization is lazy and guarded by a cached Promise so concurrent first callers do not race multiple pool constructions."
        - "Worker engines and transaction clones can inherit a parent connection manager and share pool instances; connection events are audited."
        - "A kill switch can fall back to a single-pool path."
      last_known_at: 2026-07-15
    - slug: docs/engines
      title: "Pluggable Engine Architecture"
      retrieved_via: get
      claims:
        - "PostgresEngine plus Supabase is the documented production path for multi-user or multi-device use, while PGLite is single-process/local and its documented concurrency model is limited."
        - "The BrainEngine contract includes ACID transactions, links and recursive graph traversal, raw data, versions, and ingest-log operations."
        - "Postgres uses Supavisor pooling, pgvector, tsvector/GIN, JSONB, and recursive CTEs."
      last_known_at: 2026-07-15
    - slug: docs/architecture/key_files
      title: "Key files — per-file index (gbrain repo)"
      retrieved_via: get
      claims:
        - "put_page write-through is atomic via temp-sibling plus rename."
        - "Link operations expose link_source and link_type, reject reconciliation-managed sources for manual writes, and default omitted manual provenance to manual."
        - "Remote reads are source-scoped and graph/link reads apply endpoint scoping rules."
      last_known_at: 2026-07-15
    - slug: src-core-minions-worker-ts
      title: "MinionWorker — Concurrent in-process job worker"
      retrieved_via: get
      claims:
        - "The worker processes jobs concurrently with isolated per-job state, lock renewal, token-fenced completion, stall detection, and retry handling."
      last_known_at: 2026-07-15
  known_decisions:
    - "Use PostgresEngine plus Supabase for production multi-user/multi-device GBrain deployments rather than PGLite as the shared backend."
    - "Keep reads on the pooler and long-running DDL/bulk work on a capped direct pool; allow inherited managers to share pool instances."
    - "Preserve provenance on links and use atomic write-through for local Markdown."
  gaps:
    - "No existing Brain page directly compares shared-writer, single-writer/command-log, and partitioned-writer architectures for concurrent AI agents."
    - "No direct Brain claim specifies an end-to-end conflict protocol for page versions, graph edges, facts, and provenance under concurrent agents."
    - "No direct Brain claim quantifies a strict connection budget or maps pool sizes to Supabase plan limits."
    - "No direct Brain claim establishes whether queue/worker serialization should be the sole write coordinator or only an admission-control layer."
  contradictions: []
